spans.content: Transformation from Weaire-Phelan packing to thermally stable Euler
crystals in polycrystalline face-centered cubic metals
Dan Yang a,b , Xiaoming Su c , Hongtao Xue a , Xiuyan Li b,d , Zhaohui Jin b,d,*
a State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Non-ferrous Metals, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology,
Lanzhou 730050, China
b Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 72 Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, China
c The Center for High Performance Computing, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200240, China
d Liaoning Academy of Materials, 280 Chuangxin Road, Shenyang 110167, China
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Weaire-Phelan packing
Minimal surface
Catenoid
Grain boundary
Thermal stability
Polycrystalline metal
Molecular dynamics
A B S T R A C T
Based on the least area principle to fill up space and Weaire-Phelan (W-P) conjecture, polycrystalline face-
centered cubic metals Cu, Ni and Al consisting of triply-distributed coherent twin-boundaries were modeled at
grain sizes below 10 nm. Atomistic simulations showed that grain boundaries in such W-P crystals may transform
promptly into a unique structure characterized by three perpendicular catenoids of minimal surfaces formally
proved by mathematician Leonhard Euler. The adopted Euler crystal can be thermally stable up to more than 80
of the melting point. Densely embedded Σ3 {111} coherent twins are critical agents to trigger the trans-
formation as well as to stabilize the resultant minimal-surface structures. Our results add further evidence that
the evolution of 3-dimensional grain boundary networks into saddle-shaped minimal-surface morphologies may
enhance the thermal stability of nanograined polycrystals.
1. Introduction
In nanostructured polycrystalline metals, all tiny pieces of crystals or
nanometer sized grains are surrounded by segments of crystal interfaces
or grain boundaries (GBs). The 3-dimensional (3D) GB morphology is a
complex entity because both lattice orientation and shape of individual
grains can be adopted randomly [1,2]. In addition, GBs themselves differ
from each other both structurally and energetically and as intrinsically
mobile planar defects, they may exhibit a variety of mobilities
depending on temperatures, externally applied forces and the material
[3]. It has been remained mysterious whether any unique 3D structures
may possibly form to represent somewhat optimal polycrystals with an
overall stability opposing collective structural changes, most notably,
grain coarsening and grain growth [4].
In topological terms, a polycrystalline structure can be treated as a
face-edge-vertice (FEV) network connected by GBs (faces), edges or
triple lines (TLs, where three GB planes meet) and vertices or quad-
rupolar points (QPs, where four TLs meet) [5,6]. The FEV-GB network is
useful to characterize shapes of polyhedral grains. Alternatively, in
terms of Voronoi diagram (or Dirichlet tessellation), an interpenetrating
grain-face network can be specified to characterize a GB network asso-
ciated with lattice relationship of neighboring grains. For example, if a
chain of grains is connected by parallel coherent twin boundaries (CTBs)
in one direction, we say that a channel (or tunnel) of CTB network is
formed in that direction.
The presence of twin-related low-index coincidence site lattice (CSL)
boundaries and their influence to morphological changes have been
investigated for a number of face-centered cubic (FCC) materials [7–11].
It is believed that twin boundaries in general improve the microstruc-
tural stability of polycrystalline metals. Most notably, it has been shown
that introducing 3D CTB networks to neighboring grains of Cu is
essential to trigger a structural transformation from a FEV-GB network
to a unique metastable structure called ‘diamond Schwarz crystal (D-SC)’
upon annealing [12]. The D-SC structure is characterized by smoothly
distributed 3D periodic minimal surface of Σ9-type general GBs as well
as four channels of CTB-networks in each
direction of the cubic
lattice. The structure was found extremely stable, exhibiting
near-melting point thermostability and almost theoretical mechanical
strength [12–14].
The structural stability of Schwarz crystals is promising. Yet, it

Fig. 1. Atomistic model of Weaire-Phelan crystal. (a) Dodecahedron (Dod) in cube lattice orientation and a pair of twinned Tetrakaidecahedron (Tet) with Σ3 {111}
CTBs in between (right). (b) W-P packing with two Dod cells and three pairs of perpendicular Tet cells. (c) The W-P crystal subjected to 3D periodic boundary
conditions. The supercell size of a W-P crystal is measured by a which is twice the grain size, d.
remains intriguing whether enhanced thermostability can be achieved
by incorporating CTB networks of other forms in order to prevent GBs
from developing substantial curvatures to escape. In this paper, rather
than to examine a Kelvin polycrystal resembling the ‘ideal’ foam pro-
posed by Lord Kelvin [15], we consider Weaire-Phelan (W-P) conjecture
[16] as an optimal way to divide space and construct initial nanograined
polycrystalline FCC metals of Cu, Ni and Al containing triply-distributed
perpendicular CTB channels.
Nanoscale microstructures indicating W-P tessellation have been
observed experimentally such as in sputtering epitaxial growth of Pd/Pb
monolayers [17] and in polymers due to polymerization induced phase
separation [18]. Various Frank-Kasper phases beyond the packing rules
known for metallic systems were also revealed with self-assembled
molecules in soft matters [19]. These recent experimental observa-
tions suggest that W-P packing is not merely an interesting conjecture,
but also represents a remarkable way to obtain optimized 3D micro-
structures in practice.
2. Weaire-Phelan (W-P) packing
The W-P model represents a complex 3D geometry of idealized foams
of equal-sized bubbles. It is a better solution to the “Kelvin problem”
than the Kelvin model [20,21]. The W-P structure uses two kinds of cells
of equal volume, called “tetrakaidecahedron” (Tet) and “dodecahedron”
(Dod), respectively, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). A W-P unit cell consists of
three pairs of Tets and two sets of Dods. The center coordinates for each
cell in a W-P construction follow the A15 Frank–Kasper phase [16]:
(1) Corner Dod or Dod0, (0, 0, 0); center Dod or Dod1,
( { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } , { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } , { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } )
; Tet Z:
The unique arrangements of Tets allow us to introduce twin
boundaries in 3D, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a), while the lattice orienta-
tions of Dods, in principle, can be arbitrarily chosen. Initial W-P
supercells in our MD simulations were built by considering both the size
(d) and lattice orientations of those isovolumetric grains (Tets and
Dods). The three pairs of Tets aligned in X, Y and Z directions allows one
to introduce symmetric CTBs, such as Σ3 {111} or Σ5
in X, Y and Z
directions. Dods at the corner (Dod0) and at the center (Dod1) are of the
same shape but perpendicular to each other. Obviously, the cubic ori-
entations can be assigned to the two Dods to simplify the symmetric fit.
In particular, we consider combinations of cubic Dods and twin Tets
with those lattice orientations prescribed in Table 1.
Lattice orientations assigned to W-P cells provided in Table 1 intro-
duce Σ3 and Σ5 CTB networks. Clearly, for W-P crystals with Σ3 CTBs,
GB segments of
type (sometimes called incoherent twin
boundaries, ITBs [9]) are formed between Dod0 and its neighboring
Tets; whereas GB segments of
type are formed between
Dod1 and its neighboring Tets. It is expectable that the two types of
asymmetric GB segments are not equally stable due to their different
structures. The situation does not occur to W-P crystals with Σ5 CTBs,
where all GBs segments are of
twist and asymmetric character,
so, the structure is more uniform and energetically more isotropic.
In this study, W-P cells of different sizes, i.e.
6 . 5 \mathrm { n m }

, respectively, were modelled and simulated. Due to the
geometric (shape) symmetry of the W-P crystal, rotating each pair of
Tets by 90 degrees around X, Y or Z axis results in essentially an identical

Different GB networks can also be constructed by assigning different
lattice orientations to Dods. In doing so, however, no W-P cell exhibiting
superior thermal stabilities could be found according to our MD tests.
3. MD simulations
All MD simulations were carried out with the molecular dynamics
(MD) code LAMMPS [22]. The simulation system (a triclinic supercell) is
periodic in all directions. Particle trajectories were integrated using the
Nos´e-Hoover thermostat with a timestep of 2.5 fs. An isothermal isobaric
ensemble (NPT) was used to maintain the zero-pressure condition. The
heating procedure was conducted by increasing the temperature at in-
tervals of 1 K at a heating rate of
8 ~ \mathrm { K } / \mathrm { n s }
, similar to that in Refs. 12-14.
The interatomic potential for Cu is based on a modified version of
semi-empirical model (jin_copper_lammps.setfl) developed with the
embedded atom methods (EAM) for Cu [23]. EAM potentials developed
by Mendelev et al [24] is chosen to use for Ni and a force-matching
potential based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations is cho-
sen for Al [25]. Comparing to experimental data and DFT calculations
[26–29], all the three sets potentials may reproduce fundamental ma-
terial properties reasonably well, such as elastic constants, melting point
and stacking fault energies (see Table S1 in supplemental data).
The software Ovito [30,31] was used for analyzing and visualizing
our MD data. With common neighbor analyses (CNA), atoms at stacking
faults, twin boundaries and other defects can be distinguished from
those at FCC lattice sites.

4. Simulation results
4.1. Transformation from W-P to Euler crystal and thermal stability
With Σ3 CTBs, structural evolution of W-P crystals into GB mor-
phologies resembling Euler catenoids [32] are generally observed for
MD samples of different cell sizes
\dot { \mathbf { } d } = 3 . 2 5 \mathrm { n m }
, 5 nm, 7.5 nm and
{ 1 0 } \mathrm { n m }
respectively). Taken the case of 5 nm cell size as an example (Fig. 2 and
supplemental movie clips), our observations may be summarized as
follows.
(1) The Dod at the corner (Dod0) retracts promptly at elevated
temperatures and finally into a GB node or anchor at about
4 5 0 \mathrm { K } ,
a temperature (TWP-E, Figs. 2 and 3) at which the three pairs of
twin Tets have transformed simultaneously into three ‘catenoids’
perpendicular to each other. The Dod at the center (Dod1), on the
other hand, does not alter much. The transformation can be
revealed by measuring the lateral spacing of CTBs in each pair of
twin Tets or catenoids as a function of temperature, as shown in
Fig. 3 (a) (b). The size-dependent transformation temperature has
been shown in Fig. 3 (c) for Cu.
(2) Mathematically, a Euler minimal surface for a single vertical
catenoid can be defined by [33–35]
r (z) = \sqrt {x ^ {2} + y ^ {2}} = \alpha \cosh \frac {z}{\alpha} \tag {1}
where
x , y ,

are coordinate positions and
\alpha

(evolutionary parameter). Our MD observed catenoid configuration is
found close to that described by Eq. 1 with
\scriptstyle \alpha = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } .
(3) Preexsiting CTBs in each pair of Tets never tend to migrate away
from their initial positions via mechanical twinning before and
after the transformation. The shape of the CTB area may vary and
deviate from the ideal circular one, manifesting their roles of
dragging or impinging GBs bounding them. Our MD results reveal
again that inhibiting grain growth by CTB networks depends

(Fig. 4), e.g., double or twice-double the number of CTBs (noted

Nearly the same thermal stability can be achieved by further
doubling the twin density

. For both cases, W-P crystals
were found capable to transform into Euler crystals and the

Fig. 4 (d) for Cu as well as for Ni and Al.
(5) The Dod1 at the box center is also found bounded by additional
CTBs, products of thermally activated GB reactions at tempera-
tures above room temperature, as shown in Fig. 4 and 5. GB
decomposing into twins constitute an important mode of GB ac-
tivity accounting for grain growth or coarsening according to our
MD observations.
4.2. GB networks in Euler crystals (catenoids, chains and anchor)
A Euler crystal consists of three identical catenoids in
X , ~ Y ,
and Z
directions in close contact. A complemental grain at the center bounded
by portions of embedded catenoids is also necessary to fill up the space
D. Yang et al.
Acta Materialia 296 (2025) 121289
GB network
Fig. 5. GB network in a Euler crystal and the schematic TLs and QPs. (a) GB morphology of a Euler crystal (CTB atoms in red and GB atoms in grey). (b) Schematic GB
network. (c) Super QP as the GB anchor and (d) the schematic super QP with eight chains (TLs).
of a supercell (Fig. 5 and Fig. S1 in supplemental). As can be seen, the
rims of the three catenoids are anchored at the corners of the cubic box
but framed in such a way that from the anchor toward the box center, a
triple line or a short chain connecting the anchor and the corner of the
inner grain is formed. Accordingly, ideal Euler crystals can be built using
three identical catenoids lying on X, Y, and Z planes respectively and
packing them into close contact. As verified in our MD simulations
(Fig. S1), the Euler crystals constructed this way are thermally as stable
as those transformed from W-P crystals.
Unlike W-P crystal with convex grain cells and flat GB segments,
Euler crystals consisting of concave catenoids. In terms of a FEV-
network, the minimal surface GBs of catenoids
) are connected
by 32 triple lines (TLs) and 9 quadrupolar points (QPs). Among the QPs,
eight of them are standard ones which are associated with Dod 1. The
other one, on the other hand, is a ‘super’ QP where eight TLs or chains
end up (Fig. 5 (b)). As an anchor of the three Euler catenoids linked by
eight chains, such a super QP turns out to be extremely stable according
to our MD observations.
The ultimate thermal stability of polycrystals depends on GB net-
works at equilibrium, both locally and globally. Our MD observations
show clearly that after transforming into a Euler crystal, the number of
GB nodes or edges in a W-P crystal can be reduced significantly, such
that an equilibrium GB network of zero mean-curvature catenoids, GB
chains and anchor is formed opposing thermal fluctuations and grain
coarsening at elevated temperatures.
4.3. A counter example: W-P crystals with Σ5 CTBs
No transformation to Euler catenoid was observed in our MD
simulations if a W-P crystal is composed of GB network of other types,
such as Σ5 or Σ9. Fig. 6 (a) illustrates such a case, a W-P crystal with Σ5
twin Tets
\dot { \boldsymbol { d } } = 5 . 3 \ \mathrm { n m }
). With this set of W-P crystals, we found that at
elevated temperatures, instead of retracting into an anchor, the grain
cell Dod0 neither changed its size nor its shape noticeably, while Dod1
underwent grain growth or coarsening at temperatures above the room
temperature. The process of coarsening is continuous, initially slow and
tends to be faster at temperatures beyond
4 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } ,
, such that the W-P
structure collapses promptly at about
6 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K }
when the area covered by
Σ5 CTB in twin Tets shrinks and vanishes (Fig. 6 (b)-(c), see also the
supplemental movie clip). Our MD observations revealed again that GBs
enclosing a Dod may exhibit very different mobilities at elevated
temperatures.
5. Discussion
Unlike the 2D or 3D foam geometry where the surface tension of
bubbles can be treated approximately as everywhere uniform [34,35],
an equilibrium GB network in polycrystals not only depends on the
shapes of grains, but also the texture profile of neighboring grains [2].
The reason is simple, depending on the misorientation angle of neigh-
boring grain lattices, GBs in a piece of polycrystal may show different
atomic structures, excess energies and mobilities (either intrinsic or
extrinsic).
Texture effects on structural thermostabilities due to isotropic/
anisotropic GB energies and elastic mismatch of neighboring grain lat-
tices have been considered in a recent paper for twin-free Schwarz
crystals of different shapes and sizes [36]. For GBs resembling primary,
diamond or gyroid triply periodic minimal surfaces, it is concluded that
D. Yang et al.

energetically less anisotropic GBs are expected to be thermally more
stable. Similar considerations also apply to address the transformation
from W-P to Euler crystal and the MD observed thermostability,
although the present situation is much complicated due to that
minimal-surface GBs (catenoids), CTBs, GB nodes (QPs) and GB chains
(TLs) coexist in a Euler crystal.
5.1. GB anchor
Adaptive GB migration play essential roles ensuring structural evo-
lution from W-P crystal to minimal-surface Euler catenoids. In W-P
crystals with Σ3 twin Tets, at least two different kinds of GB charac-
teristics were introduced between Dods and Tets, which are {112} /
{010} TD0 GBs between Dod0 and twin Tets and {110} / {001} TD1 GBs
between Dod1 and twin Tets, according to Fig. 1 and Table 1. The TD0
GB, similar to incoherent Σ3 twin boundaries [9], happens to be mobile
such that the cell Dod0 bounded by TD0 GBs may deviate promptly from
its initial shape at elevated temperatures (Fig. 3 and 4). It retracts and
finally merges into a GB node at the point of disappearance, anchoring
the three twin catenoids simultaneously.
At least three factors account for the distinct behaviors exhibited by

the two Dods, GB structure, GB energy and modes of GB motion. In
general, GB structures and the mode of GB motion, such as migration
and sliding, are mutually coupled [37]. It is also revealed recently that
unlike symmetric GBs, asymmetric GBs may show less-predictable mo-
bilities [38]. On the other hand, according to our MD calculations for the
three FCC metals, GB energies of TD0 can be twice larger than that of
TD1 (Table 2). Different mobilities exhibited by TD0 and TD1 are
therefore well-expected.
No such difference was observed for W-P crystals with Σ5 twin Tets.
In this case, Dod0 and Dod1 are bounded by
TD0-type
GBs and
TD1-type GBs, respectively. Our MD calcula-
tions show that the variation of GB energies is merely
between
pure-twist and symmetric tilt Σ5 planar GBs (Table 3). In other words,
GB energies between Tets and Dods fulfill more closely the isotropic GB
criterion [36] such that the evolution of the entire GB network into that
of a Euler crystal can be ruled out.
5.2. GB chains and twin decomposition
Our MD observations suggest that, in addition to zero-mean curva-
ture characteristic of Euler catenoids underlying vanishing surface ten-
sion or capillary forces driving GB migration according to the Young-
Laplace law [12,36], twin products due to GB reactions at TLs and
QPs also contribute to stabilize the GB network of a Euler crystal at
equilibrium.
GBs between Tet pairs can also be put into two categories, approxi-
mately, which can be visualized as shown in Figs. 5 and 7. Such minimal-
surface GBs follows lattice orientations of typical Σ9 GBs, such as A
[122] [212] [221] [36]. Those Σ9 GBs not only possess different excess
energies in different GB normal (supplemental Table S2), but also are
capable to decompose into twins (supplemental Fig. S2). Specifically, a
Σ9 GB is marked by ‘Σ9a’ if GB reactions into twins may occur more
readily; otherwise, it is marked by ‘Σ9b’.
According to the well-known Herring’s rule [39], the balance of GB
energies at any point of an equilibrium GB triple line in terms of the
dihedral angles (θ) is given by
\frac {\gamma_ {G B 1}}{\sin \theta_ {1}} = \frac {\gamma_ {G B 2}}{\sin \theta_ {2}} = \frac {\gamma_ {G B 3}}{\sin \theta_ {3}} \tag {2}
Therefore, it requires a mutual compromise of GB energies, dihedral
angles and the position of triple junctions to stabilize a TL out of equi-
librium. Otherwise, additional activities such as thermally activated GB
reactions may operate upon which a TL may dissociate, e.g., according
to
\Sigma 9 a + \Sigma 9 a + \Sigma 9 a {  } \Sigma 9 ^ { \prime } + \Sigma 3 + \Sigma 3 ^ { \prime } + \Sigma 3 ^ { \prime \prime } [ 2 , 6 ] .
QPs associated with
Dod1 act as energetically favorable sites to trigger GB reactions as
observed in our MD simulations (Fig. 3 and 4).
Different combinations of Σ9a and Σ9b GBs thus can be assigned for a
given chain, such that the twin decomposition is more readily to occur if
a chain is formed by more than two Σ9a GBs (e.g., a-a-a and a-a-b) and
less readily to occur if it is formed by more than two Σ9b GBs (e.g., b-b-a
and b-b-b). As illustrated in Fig. 7, such a combination rule indeed ap-
plies when additional twins are produced at elevated temperatures in
our MD simulations.
5.3. Thermal stability: MD vs. experimental data
The MD measured size-dependent thermostability for W-P crystals
and Eulers crystals of Cu, Al and Ni have been plotted in Fig. 8. Although
the transformation from W-P to Euler crystal has been observed for all
the three metals, the reduced grain coarsening temperatures are not
equivalent. Such a material dependence can be traced back to different
bonding characteristics in different FCC metals. In particular, in terms of
stable vs. unstable stacking fault energies [26], symmetric vs. asym-
metric GB energies and the orientation-dependent Young’s and shear
moduli [36], both Cu and Ni are intrinsically more anisotropic than Al.
Clearly, the isotropic characteristic of Al can be revealed in Fig. 8.
Several pieces of experimentally observed grain coarsening data [13,
40,41] are also included in Fig. 8. The reduced grain coarsening tem-
peratures suggest that enhanced thermal stability can be obtained with
grain sizes below
1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
in experiments. The trend revealed by
extension of our MD data agrees reasonably well with the experimen-
tally observed outstanding thermostability in the nanometer regime.
According to our MD data, topologically arrested GB network and pro-
moted thermal stability of polycrystals are closely relevant and
conceivable for nanograined polycrystals.
6. Summary
We summarize our results and conclude as following.
(i) Inspired by W-P geometry, the polycrystalline structure of Cu was
modeled by mixing two types of grain cells of equal volume but
different shapes, tessellating space with an optimized global
minimization of GB area. Vertical CTB networks (Voronoi) were
introduced to clarify their roles on the thermal stability of W-P
crystals at the grain size limit below 10 nm via MD simulations.
(ii) Structural evolution of W-P crystals into Euler crystals charac-
terized by catenoids with minimal-surface GBs were observed,
which occurs in W-P crystals with Σ3 {111} twin Tets. The
transformation ensures optimized GB morphologies both struc-
turally and energetically, resulting in metastable states of poly-
crystalline microstructures at equilibrium.
(iii) The structural transformation depends on equilibrium conditions
of surface tensions (GB energies), balanced by GB migration as
well as GB reactions to produce additional twins, preferentially at
GB nodes and chains (QPs and TLs). The scenarios suggest that Σ9
related asymmetric GBs are in general highly adaptative in FCC
polycrystals.
(iv) Twin boundary networks and their distribution profiles are
essential to promote the transformation to topological minimal-
surface structures. The ultimate thermal stability of a GB
D. Yang et al.
Acta Materialia 296 (2025) 121289
Euler-CTB
B
D'
Fig. 7. GB reactions in (a) a Euler crystal depends on (b) the combination of two types of Σ9 GBs (Σ9a and Σ9b). MD snapshots taken in (c) [110] direction and (d) in
[1-10] direction at different temperatures.
network relies on CTB locking and fencing effects and hence
depends on the extent of enclosed twins.
(v) Besides of the prototype minimal-surface structure found previ-
ously based on Kelvin conjecture, i.e., the diamond Schwarz
crystals (D-SCs) [12], our MD simulations provided further evi-
dence that the presence of minimal-surface GB in other topolog-
ical forms, such as Euler catenoid, is also possible.
Achieving desired stability of microstructure is key issue for mate-
rials engineering [42–45]. Proper combination of grain lattice orienta-
tions is essential to stabilize the microstructure of nanograined
polycrystals. Promoting microstructural evolution into Schwarz crystals
including Euler catenoids through texture-selective processing tech-
niques can be considered as a new strategy to optimize and design
polycrystalline metals and alloys with improved thermal stabilities for
various engineering purposes.
